body_txt: A Stock Aesop about living your life and fighting against all odds. Or "enjoy the day", "make good use of the day", or, more literally, "harvest the day", as Carpe Diem's meaning ( in Latin ) goes—don't waste time, enjoy the present, live life instead of letting it pass you by. (The commonly seen "seize the day" is a mistranslation, as explained below.) Generally used as an encouragement for people stuck in their problems and thinking they can't enjoy life. This is the common purpose of a Manic Pixie Dream Girl to the guy she's close with. On the other hand, sometimes this Aesop can be warped into "anything can happen to us, so be crazy" - literally (read: Sex, Drugs, and Rock & Roll ). The Hedonist is likely to use this idea to justify living only for immediate pleasures. The saying YOLO (you only live once) is the epitome of said warping. This is contrasted with another saying tempus fugit (time flies away), which is about using your time for most productivity and not wasting it on useless things. A specific type of Life-Affirming Aesop , in that life is not just worth living but requires us to make an effort to get the most out of it. Compare The Anti-Nihilist and We All Die Someday (aka memento mori - remember [about] death). See also We Are as Mayflies , which may lead someone to seize the day. Related to Like You Were Dying , when someone’s impending passing inspires them to make the most of the time they have left. Also may overlap with "Life Is Awesome" Speech , a more general affirmation that our time here is a precious commodity. Not to be confused with the webcomic Carpe Diem . The common mistranslation seize the day likely occurred to a misunderstanding of the third-conjugation singular imperative: Thus, the correct Latin for seize the day is cape diem, not carpe diem . ( Harvest the day is actually a perfectly defensible translation of carpe diem in the context of the poem it's quoted from - see the Poetry section below. Also, the Latin word cape is pronounced roughly how a Bostonian would say "car pay": KAH-pay .)
